curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III Volume 2, Portfolio Construction, Learning Module 7

drills.los_text: discuss financial risks associated with the portfolio strategy of an institutional investor
drills.reading_name: Case Study in Portfolio Management: Institutional (SWF)

questions.stem: R-SWF (Republic of Ruritania SWF) is a 'savings fund' *most likely* intended to:

options.A: share wealth across generations by transforming non-renewable assets (rare earth metals deposit sold ~20 years ago) into diversified financial assets.
options.B: R-SWF is a savings fund intended to provide immediate cash distributions to Ruritania's citizens each year.
options.C: R-SWF exists to fund the government's current operating budget on an annual basis.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading's case background: 'Over 20 years ago, the "Republic of Ruritania" discovered an extremely large deposit of crucial rare earth metals... The government of Ruritania (R) decided to form a sovereign wealth fund, R-SWF, in order to grow the capital for future generations. This type of SWF is a "savings fund," intended to share wealth across generations by transforming non-renewable assets into diversified financial assets.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Immediate distribution is a different fund type; R-SWF transforms depleted resource wealth into diversified assets for future generations.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Budget funding describes a stabilization or fiscal fund; the savings mandate is intergenerational wealth sharing.

questions.stem: R-SWF's asset allocation at Memo 1A shows Alternatives at *most likely*:

options.A: Memo 1A shows R-SWF's Alternatives allocation at 10%, a small satellite position.
options.B: 50% of the $50 billion fund — reflecting long-term orientation, with within-Alternatives split of Real Estate 15%, Infrastructure 10%, Private Capital 10%, Hedge Funds 10%, Natural Resources 5%.
options.C: Memo 1A shows R-SWF at 90% Alternatives, an essentially all-alternatives portfolio.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The allocation is 50% of the $50 billion fund, not a satellite sleeve.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 50% of the $50 billion fund — reflecting long-term orientation, with within-Alternatives split of Real Estate 15%, Infrastructure 10%, Private Capital 10%, Hedge Funds 10%, Natural Resources 5%; equity 40%, FI 9%, cash 1%.

Correct. Per Memo 1A: 'As of last month, R-SWF had approximately 50% of assets invested in alternative investments, consistent with its long-term objectives.' Total Portfolio: Alternatives 50%, Equities 40%, Fixed Income 9%, Cash 1%. Alternatives breakdown: Real Estate 15%, Infrastructure 10%, Private Capital 10%, Hedge Funds 10%, Natural Resources 5%.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Half, not nearly all — with the internal split across Real Estate (15%), Infrastructure, PE, and other categories.

questions.stem: Per the reading, R-SWF's Risk Analyst *most likely* expects *liquidity risk* to be:

options.A: the top risk highlighted for both investments.
options.B: unmeasurable.
options.C: NOT highlighted as significant risk for either investment.

rationales.A: Not the case here.
rationales.B: Liquidity is measurable.
rationales.C:
Full statement: NOT highlighted as significant risk for either investment — because each is a small portion of R-SWF's total assets (0.2% and 0.05%), R-SWF has no cash flow pressure (unlike pension funds/insurers with regular liability payments), and direct investments provide more control over exit timing.

Correct. Per the reading's in-text answer: 'No. I do not anticipate the Head of Infrastructure or the Head of PE to highlight liquidity risk as a significant risk for either investment. Although liquidity risk is the main risk that long-term institutional investors face, particularly during a significant market decline, each of these investments represents a small portion of R-SWF's total assets. R-SWF does not have cash flow pressure, unlike many institutional investors that face pressure from the regular payment of liabilities. In addition, R-SWF has been growing over time and is making a concerted effort to expand its direct investment program. Direct investments typically help mitigate some of the liquidity issues commonly experienced when investing in a fund because direct investment provides a greater amount of control and discretion over when to exit investments.'

questions.stem: The Sunnyland airport BOT (build-operate-transfer) investment structure *most likely* involves:

options.A: $500M total project cost, with $100M from R-SWF (0.2% of AUM), $33M from AOG (airport operator), $300M non-recourse project finance debt (~70/30 debt/equity) with 15-year tenor + 3-year grace period, 2-year construction period, 25-year concession, 35% quarterly concession fee to SAA on all revenue, expected 15% IRR.
options.B: In the Sunnyland airport BOT structure, R-SWF funds 100% of the $500 million project cost alone, taking full equity ownership without co-investors or external financing.
options.C: The airport project uses no leverage of any kind, funded entirely with equity contributions from the sponsoring investors in proportion to their ownership stakes.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Memo 1B: Total project cost $500M for 5M pax terminal. $100M from R-SWF (0.2% AUM), $33M from AOG (fixed fee plus/minus performance incentive), $300M non-recourse project finance debt (approx 70/30 debt/equity) with 15-year tenor following 3-year grace period. 2-year construction, 25-year concession (including 2-year construction). 35% quarterly concession fee to SAA on all revenue. Airport charges (70% of revenue) regulated by concession contract, subject to CPI-type formula. Expected IRR 15% for full 25-year term.
rationales.B: Incorrect. R-SWF contributes $100M of a $500M project (0.2% of AUM) alongside AOG's $33M and other participants.
rationales.C: Incorrect. $300M of non-recourse project financing provides substantial leverage.

questions.stem: The ABC (Atsui Beverage Company) PE investment *most likely* involves:

options.A: R-SWF taking control.
options.B: $25M for 35% minority stake — 0.05% of total assets, 0.4% of total PE assets.
options.C: $100M for full ownership.

rationales.A: Minority stake.
rationales.B:
Full statement: $25M for 35% minority stake — 0.05% of total assets, 0.4% of total PE assets; founder retains 65% and is worried about giving up control; $20M for plant modernization; R-SWF will get 2 board seats (from 5 to 7).

Correct. Per Memo 1C: 'ABC is keen on modernizing its plant, but the founder is worried about giving up control. Thus, the founder is willing to sell only a minority stake of 35% in exchange for $25 million. For R-SWF (with assets of $50 billion), this is a small investment (0.05% of total assets). The investment will be about 0.4% of total PE assets... With a significant minority, R-SWF will be allowed to have two seats on the board of ABC. So, the board will expand from five members to seven members. R-SWF is planning to have the Head of PE join the board of ABC but hasn't decided on the other board seat.' Revenue $50M, EBITDA $7M (14% margin), 10x EBITDA multiple.
rationales.C: Only 35%.

questions.stem: The Head of Risk *most likely* raises which key risk regarding the ABC investment tariffs?

options.A: The Head of Risk raises no concern about tariffs in connection with the ABC investment.
options.B: The Head of Risk's tariff concern is that regulation will tighten further, raising ABC's protection.
options.C: the risk that entry barriers (tariffs) fall away after the upcoming election — ABC's competitive advantage relies on 100% tariff on foreign beverages.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Tariffs are the central identified vulnerability.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The risk runs the other way — the 100% tariff wall could FALL after the election, dissolving ABC's competitive advantage.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the risk that entry barriers (tariffs) fall away after the upcoming election — ABC's competitive advantage relies on 100% tariff on foreign beverages; if reduced, foreign giants (Coke, Pepsi) with deeper pockets could enter and price war.

Correct. Per the reading's Investment Committee dialogue: 'Head of Risk: These do seem like heavy tariffs. CIO mentioned they're as high as 100% if you try to buy Coke or Pepsi. The memo says there's an election coming up. Surely there's a risk those entry barriers fall away?' Head of Equities later adds: 'Let's apply a probability to a tariff reduction and to import markets opening up. Pepsi and Coca-Cola have much deeper pockets for waiting out a price war.'

questions.stem: For the Sunnyland airport project, *currency risk* on the *revenue side* is *most likely* mitigated because:

options.A: 70% of revenue = airport charges (typically outsourced to IATA collection, paid in USD by airlines).
options.B: not mitigated at all.
options.C: the local currency is stable.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 70% of revenue = airport charges (typically outsourced to IATA collection, paid in USD by airlines); remaining 30% commercial = in tourist locale so priced in USD; overall the revenue side is mostly USD-denominated.

Correct. Per the Investment Committee dialogue: 'Head of Infrastructure: I can speak to the currency risk in Sunnyland. When we're talking about the aviation industry and airports, a lot of revenues for infrastructure investors come in the form of regulated charges. Look at our own concession contract: 70% of the revenues are airport charges. It's typical with these arrangements to outsource the collection of these charges to international organizations like IATA. They collect the revenue from the airline, and almost all of that is paid in dollars, so we're comfortable there. That leaves the 30% of our revenue coming from commercial sources... In the big international airports, those transactions take place in the local currency, but we're in a locale that's expressly seeking international tourism. Pricing will be geared to international markets, so we'll have the freedom to price everything in dollars.'
rationales.B: It IS mitigated.
rationales.C: Structured USD pricing.

questions.stem: For the Sunnyland project, *debt-side* currency risk is *most likely*:

options.A: domestic Sunnyland currency debt.
options.B: primarily USD-denominated ($300M non-recourse debt).
options.C: unhedged.

rationales.A: Not local currency.
rationales.B:
Full statement: primarily USD-denominated ($300M non-recourse debt) — big international banks are the lenders because local Sunnyland banks lack capacity for such large, long-term financing.

Correct. Per the dialogue: 'Head of Risk: What about the borrowing side, though? To keep people happy and the logistics simple, I assume any borrowings will come from local banks that use their country's currency. Head of Infrastructure: It's a good thought, but no. The lenders are big international banks. The in-country banks may participate, but given the size of the loans and how long term these arrangements are — at least in Sunnyland — the local banks just don't have the capacity yet. Whoever the lenders are, they'll be comfortable knowing the investors are getting their returns mostly in US dollars, which is what the $300 million of debt is denominated in.'
rationales.C: Naturally hedged via USD revenue.

questions.stem: For a frontier market direct PE investment like ABC, the *currency risk view* stated by the Head of PE is *most likely*:

options.A: fully hedged.
options.B: impossible.
options.C: 'not stressed' because visitors and most tourists use USD in Atsui.

rationales.A: Not hedged.
rationales.B: It exists though it's difficult.
rationales.C:
Full statement: 'not stressed' because visitors and most tourists use USD in Atsui — but the Head of Risk implies this view understates the difficulty of hedging frontier market currencies (which can move wildly against the USD).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of Risk: The first thing that comes to my mind when we're investing in frontier markets like these is, "How do we deal with the currency risk?" It's hard to hedge these currencies. Meanwhile, they can move wildly against the dollar, turning a really good investment into a really bad investment. What's your read on this, Head of PE? Head of PE: I'm not stressed about it. When it comes to me and most other visitors to Atsui, we're using US dollars.' At the 3-year update, a currency devaluation happens, validating Head of Risk's concern.

questions.stem: Sensitivity analysis on the Sunnyland airport *IRR* *most likely* shows:

options.A: base case 15% IRR; low-traffic case 10-11%.
options.B: IRR always 15%.
options.C: IRR always 25%.

rationales.A:
Full statement: base case 15% IRR; low-traffic case 10-11%; high-traffic case high teens; ±10% CapEx sensitivity around a fixed-price contract impacts IRR modestly; real cost overruns and delays could reduce IRR to about 13%.

Correct. Per the reading's Head of Infrastructure: 'Our analysis of the reasonable low case puts IRR down to around 10% or 11%. The high case pushes the return out into the high teens. There are some sensitivities around CapEx, and we're looking to manage this risk through a fixed-price contract, the language of which says that whatever penalties we'd face for delayed or subpar construction will be passed down to the contractor. We've applied a ±10% sensitivity around that, and it does impinge on the IRR a little bit but not as much as the low-traffic case. If we run into real cost overruns or delays, we're looking at about a 13% IRR.'
rationales.B: Range of outcomes exists.
rationales.C: Not 25%.

questions.stem: Traffic (tourism) risk is the *biggest financial risk* for the airport BOT because *most likely*:

options.A: traffic doesn't matter.
options.B: the airport's cash flows depend on the actual number of visitors.
options.C: traffic is stable.

rationales.A: Traffic drives revenue.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the airport's cash flows depend on the actual number of visitors — pandemic risk, recession risk, and long-term climate risk (sea level rise, extreme heat) all could substantially depress traffic and revenue over the 25-year concession.

Correct. Per the dialogue: 'Head of Infrastructure: From our perspective, the biggest risk is traffic — comparing the actual number of visitors and tourists coming in and out of the airport against our projections... Head of Equities: We need to consider the risk of a prolonged global recession when discretionary vacations and spending take a nosedive. For a small island like Sunnyland, this is a big risk. Head of Risk: We all experienced the coronavirus pandemic in 2020, and plenty of scientists have warned us that pandemics are going to be more likely.' Reduced traffic → reduced airport charges (70% of revenue) and commercial revenue.
rationales.C: Traffic can be volatile.

questions.stem: The airport's *debt-service reserve* and *non-recourse* project financing structure *most likely* provide:

options.A: The debt-service reserve and non-recourse structure provide no protection against revenue disruption.
options.B: The reserve and non-recourse structure provide unlimited protection for as long as any disruption lasts.
options.C: coverage for a period of time in case of temporary revenue disruption (e.g., pandemic) — beyond that period, restructuring or rescheduling of financing may be needed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The reserve exists precisely to bridge temporary revenue interruptions.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Coverage is TIME-LIMITED — beyond the reserve period, restructuring or rescue capital becomes necessary.
rationales.C:
Full statement: coverage for a period of time in case of temporary revenue disruption (e.g., pandemic) — beyond that period, restructuring or rescheduling of financing may be needed; non-recourse structure limits R-SWF's downside as equity investor.

Correct. Per the Head of Infrastructure: 'The built-in debt-service reserve covers us for a period of time, and if travel is on hold for too much longer, then we turn to restructuring or rescheduling the financing. But let's understand that the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 was a game-changer, and the language and dynamics of certain contractual agreements were adjusted to avoid straight defaults in these cases. And the concern here is about short-term impact, whereas over 25 years, we expect things to gradually recover.' Non-recourse debt limits equity holder exposure to project cash flows only.

questions.stem: At the 3-year update, the *ABC investment* faces which financial risks *most likely*?

options.A: regional recession, currency devaluation anticipated, tariffs reduced 100%→20% (loss of cost advantage), labor law restrictions (2 years' notice for terminations), slowing sales.
options.B: At the three-year update, ABC's financial risk picture is unchanged from the initial investment view.
options.C: At the three-year update, ABC's operating conditions have improved across the board.

rationales.A:
Full statement: regional recession, currency devaluation anticipated, tariffs reduced 100%→20% (loss of cost advantage), labor law restrictions (2 years' notice for terminations), slowing sales; multiple risks materialized.

Correct. Per Memo 2C: 'Atsui and surrounding nations went into a recession last year. Furthermore, a currency devaluation is anticipated. Beverages are considered a luxury item in Atsui. A new government was elected in Atsui last year and took office in January. One of the first orders of business was to reduce tariffs on imported beverages from a 100% tariff to a 20% tariff. This change hurts our cost advantage over foreign brands. Because of the increased direct investing capabilities and modern equipment... labor laws are strict in Atsui. In order to terminate the employment of an Atsui citizen, significant notice (two years) is required.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Conditions deteriorated significantly on several fronts simultaneously.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The update catalogs negatives — regional recession, anticipated currency devaluation, tariff protection cut from 100% to 20%, and restrictive labor rules.

questions.stem: For managing *higher-than-forecast future borrowing interest rates* for the Sunnyland airport, R-SWF *most likely*:

options.A: should just accept the risk.
options.B: can hedge its interest rate exposure for the Sunnyland airport project.
options.C: has no option.

rationales.A: Hedging is available.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading's guideline answer: 'R-SWF can manage the risk that actual future (borrowing) interest rates will be higher than forecast by hedging its interest rate exposure for the Sunnyland airport project.' Interest rate risk can be hedged via interest rate swaps or other instruments; long-dated project debt is particularly sensitive to rate risk.
rationales.C: Hedging is an option.

questions.stem: At the 5-year update, the Sunnyland airport *most likely* faces:

options.A: outperformance vs base case.
options.B: no material change.
options.C: continued poor performance.

rationales.A: Financial risks materialized.
rationales.B: Substantial deterioration.
rationales.C:
Full statement: continued poor performance: revenue 50% lower than base case (fewer tourists), costs 50% higher than base case (mitigating flood damage), medium/long-term forecast not promising.

Correct. Per the Year 5 update: 'The infrastructure investment continues to perform poorly because of a combination of the following: lower revenue (fewer tourists) vs forecast (50% lower than base case), higher costs (mitigating flood damage) vs forecast (50% higher than base case). The medium- and long-term forecast on this investment does not look promising.' This validates the Head of Equities' climate risk concern raised initially and the Head of Risk's traffic risk concern.

drills.los_text: discuss environmental and social risks associated with the portfolio strategy of an institutional investor
drills.reading_name: Case Study in Portfolio Management: Institutional (SWF)

questions.stem: *Physical climate risks* to real assets *most likely* include:

options.A: rising sea levels causing flooding and impacting rents/property valuation for coastal properties.
options.B: none.
options.C: only regulatory.

rationales.A:
Full statement: rising sea levels causing flooding and impacting rents/property valuation for coastal properties; prolonged extreme heat negatively affecting useful life of roads/train tracks (accelerated depreciation); large-scale weather events (hurricanes, wildfires) causing large drawdowns and making insurance difficult or expensive.

Correct. Per the reading's Impact on Real Assets: 'Rising sea levels that lead to flooding would impact both rents and property valuation for hitherto prime coastal properties. Prolonged exposure to extreme heat would negatively affect the useful life of roads and train tracks, which would lead to accelerated depreciation of such assets and, therefore, more frequent replacement costs for companies and governments. Similarly, physical damage caused by frequent, large-scale weather-related events, such as hurricanes or even wildfires — once considered too irregular to insure against — could not only lead to large-scale drawdowns in the portfolio's asset value but also make it difficult or expensive to insure such assets. Most of the flooding-related losses around the world are uninsured.'
rationales.B: Physical risks are real.
rationales.C: Physical impacts are broader.

questions.stem: *Climate transition risks* *most likely* include:

options.A: The transition to a low-carbon economy creates no investment risks.
options.B: risks from policy responses (carbon pricing, coal phase-outs, ICE sales bans), technological displacement, and stranded assets.
options.C: Climate transition risks are the same thing as physical climate risks.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Policy shifts, technological displacement, and stranded assets are real transition risks.
rationales.B:
Full statement: risks from policy responses (carbon pricing, coal phase-outs, ICE sales bans), technological displacement, and stranded assets — driven by 2015 Paris Agreement target of limiting warming to 2°C which requires 25% CO2 emissions reduction by 2030 and net zero by 2070.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In line with the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement, countries and companies around the world are already making efforts to dramatically reduce or eliminate their CO2 emissions in order to limit the global temperature increase in this century to 2 degrees Celsius above preindustrial levels. To keep global warming less than 2°C, scientists project that energy-related CO2 emissions need to fall 25% by 2030 and reach "net zero" by 2070.' Transition risks include the effects of coal phase-outs, ICE sales bans, carbon pricing (US$40-80/tCO2 by 2030 for first movers), and other policy shifts per PRI IPR forecasts.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Transition risks (carbon pricing, coal phase-outs, ICE bans) are distinct from physical risks (storms, floods, heat).

questions.stem: The Sunnyland airport project *most likely* faces which key environmental risk raised by the Head of Equities?

options.A: no environmental risk.
options.B: only construction risk.
options.C: the new runway is less than 1 km from the sea.

rationales.A: Substantial climate exposure.
rationales.B: Environmental is central.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the new runway is less than 1 km from the sea; over the 25-year investment horizon, sea-level rise and increased flooding risk could impair operations — 'Sea-level rise may be gradual, but it doesn't stop.'

Correct. Per the Head of Equities: 'If you look at the World Economic Forum's "Global Risk Report" since 2017, climate risk and extreme weather feature in the top risks every time. Year over year, the weather gets more erratic. Sea-level rise may be gradual, but it doesn't stop. And while I understand the need to support Sunnyland's economy by expanding the airport, the memo says that the new runway is less than a kilometer from the sea. Sure, you get a fantastic view when you take off and land, but the sea is rising, and the risk of flooding could become real even just during high tide. Running an airport in those conditions would not be possible. It's a 25-year infrastructure investment. That's long enough for climate risks to materialize and impact operations.'

questions.stem: The Head of Infrastructure's *initial dismissal* of climate risk is *most likely*:

options.A: based on experts' assurance that risks are unlikely to materialize in the time frame + engineered mitigation (drainage for 50-year and 100-year flood scenarios) — but the 3-year update shows climate change has led to rising seas and more frequent storms.
options.B: The Head of Infrastructure's initial dismissal of climate risk is fully validated at the three-year update.
options.C: The initial climate-risk dismissal is irrelevant to the case's lessons.

rationales.A:
Full statement: based on experts' assurance that risks are unlikely to materialize in the time frame + engineered mitigation (drainage for 50-year and 100-year flood scenarios) — but the 3-year update shows climate change has led to rising seas and more frequent storms; the initial comfort was misplaced.

Correct. Per the reading initially: 'Head of Infrastructure: what I'd say to the committee is that the experts tells us, in the time frame we're looking at, environmental risks are unlikely to materialize, and even so, they're accounted for during the design process. The drainage systems are modified to handle increases in groundwater levels, and the engineers are building in once-in-50-years and once-in-100-years flood scenarios. Those are risks they're confident they can build for.' But at 3-year update: 'The Sunnyland government is happy with the progress... However, climate change has led to rising seas and more frequent storms. Storms are common in island nations; however, the rising seas are of concern. In addition, hotter temperatures are of additional concern.' Then at 5-year: 50% higher costs from mitigating flood damage.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Events do NOT validate the dismissal — the update revisits it critically.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The dismissal (expert assurances plus 50-year drainage engineering) is central to the case's risk-governance lesson.

questions.stem: The *social risks* raised by the ABC investment 3-year update include *most likely*:

options.A: The ABC three-year update raises no social concerns.
options.B: sensitivity around firing factory workers in a frontier market during recession (labor requires 2 years' notice), reputational risk of SWF cutting jobs, community impact.
options.C: The update's concerns are exclusively environmental, with no social dimension.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Workforce and community issues are prominent.
rationales.B:
Full statement: sensitivity around firing factory workers in a frontier market during recession (labor requires 2 years' notice), reputational risk of SWF cutting jobs, community impact — 'Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs in XYZ during a Recession' would be a bad headline.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of PE: For us as a sovereign wealth fund, there's reputational risk. Flying in from world cities and firing factory employees in frontier markets mean bad publicity, especially in Atsui and especially during the recession... labor laws are strict in Atsui. In order to terminate the employment of an Atsui citizen, significant notice (two years) is required. Plus, there is reputational risk for R-SWF for firing factory employees in a frontier market during a recession.' CIO adds: 'Imagine the headline: "Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs in XYZ during a Recession."'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Firing factory workers in a frontier-market recession — with two years' notice required — is a social and reputational risk alongside the environmental issues.

questions.stem: The environmental risk from ABC's cost-cutting *most likely* involves:

options.A: ABC's cost-cutting created no environmental impact of any kind.
options.B: The environmental issue at ABC involves toxic chemical contamination of groundwater.
options.C: dumping (non-toxic) waste into the nearby river rather than transporting for proper treatment — spoiling the fishing spot.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Real damage occurred to a community resource.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The waste was NON-toxic — but dumping it in the river instead of treating it spoiled the local fishing spot.
rationales.C:
Full statement: dumping (non-toxic) waste into the nearby river rather than transporting for proper treatment — spoiling the fishing spot; scientists observe the plant site and river overlap with the range of a rare reptile found in only one other place; local endangered species issue attracting broader environmental attention.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In order to make up for lower profits (due to the above reasons), plant management has started to cut corners to save on costs. Unfortunately, one way to do this was to dump waste into the nearby river rather than transport the waste for proper treatment. Although the waste is not toxic, it is starting to spoil the lovely fishing spot near the factory... Scientists are saying that the plant site and the river overlap with the range of a rare reptile that is found here and only one other place on earth. So our site has attracted the attention of people with no interest in soda or mangoes.'

questions.stem: Other *social risks* from ABC cost-cutting include *most likely*:

options.A: reducing employee breaks from 1 hour to 30 minutes.
options.B: no impact.
options.C: wage increases.

rationales.A:
Full statement: reducing employee breaks from 1 hour to 30 minutes; removing soap from restrooms (requesting employees bring their own) — 'stuff like this can have a reputational impact.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Another way ABC has tried to cut costs is by reducing employee breaks from one hour to 30 minutes and removing soap from the restrooms, requesting that employees bring their own... I know we're a $56 billion sovereign wealth fund, and here we are talking about removing soap from a few bathrooms in the tropics where we have a $25 million direct investment, but stuff like this can have a reputational impact.' Small-dollar decisions can have outsized reputational effects.
rationales.B: Real social costs.
rationales.C: Opposite direction.

questions.stem: The reading identifies the *supply chain risk* for major consumer brands *most likely* includes:

options.A: no reputational concern.
options.B: labor management lapses (Nike, Gap, Apple, Samsung have all been accused of various levels of supply chain lapses).
options.C: no financial concerns.

rationales.A: Real reputational risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: labor management lapses (Nike, Gap, Apple, Samsung have all been accused of various levels of supply chain lapses) — significant damage to brands, reputational risk, consumer boycotts, additional costs/fines from product recalls or ad hoc supply chain shifts.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Large brands in the apparel industry, such as Nike and Gap, and in the technology space, such as Apple and Samsung, have all been accused of various levels of lapses in their supply chain related to the aforementioned labor management issues. Apart from suffering significant damage to their brands and reputations, which could lead to consumer boycotts, such companies may also face additional costs and/or fines related to product recalls and ad hoc shifting of supply chains.'
rationales.C: Both reputational and financial.

questions.stem: For SWFs with equity exposure to large apparel brands and branded tech hardware, ESG considerations for supply chain issues are *most likely*:

options.A: Supply chain ESG issues are unimportant for SWFs holding large apparel and tech hardware brands.
options.B: Supply chain ESG issues are material only for retailers, not brand owners or their investors.
options.C: of paramount importance — lack of transparency and lapses in labor management may weigh heavily on supply chain resilience amid global-scale disruptions (like COVID-19).

rationales.A: Incorrect. For these exposures the issues are of paramount importance.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Brand owners bear the reputational and resilience risk — opacity and labor lapses in supplier networks weigh directly on their equity.
rationales.C:
Full statement: of paramount importance — lack of transparency and lapses in labor management may weigh heavily on supply chain resilience amid global-scale disruptions (like COVID-19); reputational risks may arise from perception that the SWF implicitly supports improper business practices.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For SWFs with equity exposure to some of the largest apparel brands and branded tech hardware companies, considering such issues while making investments is of paramount importance because lack of transparency in the supply chain and lapses in labor management may weigh heavily on the resilience of such supply chains amid global-scale disruptions, such as that caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. In addition to the financial risks, reputational risks may also arise because of a view that the SWF implicitly supports such improper and unethical business practices.'

questions.stem: The *just transition* concept is *most likely*:

options.A: the need to ensure that there are limited negative social impacts in the pursuit of positive environmental impacts (via avoiding fossil fuels, sustainable agriculture and business practices).
options.B: The just transition concept holds that transition impacts on workers and communities may be ignored.
options.C: The just transition concept requires prioritizing environmental outcomes over people in all cases.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the need to ensure that there are limited negative social impacts in the pursuit of positive environmental impacts (via avoiding fossil fuels, sustainable agriculture and business practices) — recognizes challenges like worker displacement in coal mining and fossil fuel-based generation, and higher energy costs from carbon taxes affecting poor households.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Sustainable development involves meeting the needs of the present generation without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs... In the transition to environmentally sustainable economies and societies, several challenges may arise — for example, displacement of workers and job losses in certain industries, such as coal mining, fossil fuel extraction/production, and fossil fuel-based power generation. Similarly, increased energy costs due to carbon taxes and higher costs of commodities partly resulting from sustainable production practices may have adverse effects on the incomes of poor households. Therefore, a "just" transition is necessary to ensure that there are limited negative social impacts in our pursuit of positive environmental impacts.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Attending to those impacts is the concept's entire content.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concept BALANCES both — limiting negative social impacts while pursuing positive environmental ones.

questions.stem: *Climate mitigation opportunities* *most likely* include investment in:

options.A: Climate mitigation opportunities consist only of expanding investment in fossil fuel production.
options.B: clean energy (wind, solar, small hydro), energy efficiency, batteries and storage, smart grids, and materials (copper, battery-grade lithium).
options.C: No investable climate mitigation opportunities exist for institutional investors.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Fossil expansion runs opposite to mitigation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: clean energy (wind, solar, small hydro), energy efficiency, batteries and storage, smart grids, and materials (copper, battery-grade lithium) — companies positioned to benefit from efforts to curb long-term climate change.

Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 12: Climate Mitigation Opportunity Examples include: Clean energy (wind, solar, small hydro generation + manufacturers of windmills and solar panels); Energy efficiency (energy-efficient transportation and building solutions, recycling); Batteries and storage (improved battery capacity/efficiency); Smart grids (digitally enhanced electricity grids); Materials (copper, battery-grade lithium — key ingredients in clean energy value chain).
rationales.C: Incorrect. The opportunity set is broad — clean energy, efficiency, storage, smart grids, and transition materials like copper and battery-grade inputs.

questions.stem: *Climate adaptation opportunities* *most likely* include:

options.A: no adaptation possible.
options.B: only mitigation.
options.C: companies that help better adjust to actual or expected future climate change with an aim to reduce vulnerability.

rationales.A: Adaptation is essential.
rationales.B: Adaptation is separate from mitigation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: companies that help better adjust to actual or expected future climate change with an aim to reduce vulnerability — e.g., sustainable agriculture (improving productivity, reducing resource use).

Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 13: 'This category includes companies that would help better adjust to actual or expected future change in climate with an aim to reduce vulnerability to the harmful effects of climate change, such as food insecurity, sea-level rise, and frequent extreme weather events. Climate Adaptation Opportunity Examples include: Sustainable agriculture — Companies in this segment are involved in providing products that improve agriculture productivity and reduce the resource...'

questions.stem: Per the reading, the Task force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommends *most likely*:

options.A: organizations (banks, asset managers, asset owners) use climate-related scenario analysis to estimate implications of risks/opportunities over time.
options.B: The TCFD recommends that organizations avoid climate-scenario analysis as too speculative for disclosure.
options.C: The TCFD recommends static single-point climate forecasts in place of scenario analysis.

rationales.A:
Full statement: organizations (banks, asset managers, asset owners) use climate-related scenario analysis to estimate implications of risks/opportunities over time — e.g., analyzing 2°C, 3°C, or 4°C warmer world scenarios.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Given the uncertainty around the precise timing and magnitude of the impact of climate change, organizations are increasingly using climate-related scenario analysis to better understand how their businesses might perform under a variety of global warming scenarios — for example, in a world that is 2°C, 3°C, or 4°C warmer. The Task force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommends organizations, including banks, asset managers, and asset owners, use scenario analysis to estimate the implications of such risks and opportunities for their businesses over time and also to inform their strategic thinking. The International Energy Agency and the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change both publicly offer a set of climate-related scenarios that are widely used.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Scenario analysis is the TCFD's central recommended tool.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Multiple scenarios — not single-point forecasts — are recommended for estimating risk and opportunity implications.

questions.stem: The reading's *Exhibit 11* (Lazard 2019 Levelized Cost of Energy) *most likely* shows:

options.A: The Lazard levelized-cost data show renewables always more expensive than conventional generation.
options.B: wind and utility-scale solar PV are cost competitive with conventional generation like coal — wind $28-54, solar PV thin-film utility $32-42 vs coal $66-152 (all unsubsidized $/MWh).
options.C: The Lazard data show no cost differences among generation technologies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The data show the OPPOSITE for wind and utility-scale solar.
rationales.B:
Full statement: wind and utility-scale solar PV are cost competitive with conventional generation like coal — wind $28-54, solar PV thin-film utility $32-42 vs coal $66-152 (all unsubsidized $/MWh); this cost competitiveness plus urgency to decarbonize created secular growth opportunity.

Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 11 (Levelized cost of energy — Lazard 2019, $/MWh unsubsidized): Wind $28-54, Solar PV thin-film utility $32-42, Gas Combined Cycle $44-68, Coal $66-152, Nuclear $118-192. 'This cost competitiveness, coupled with the urgency to decarbonize our economies to avoid the potentially catastrophic physical impacts of climate change, has created secular growth opportunity for such businesses and assets, thereby attracting increasingly large investor attention.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Differences are material — wind at $28-54 and utility-scale solar PV compete directly with coal.

questions.stem: For the SWF making the ABC investment, the *social license to operate* concept *most likely* means:

options.A: regulatory approval only.
options.B: no community engagement.
options.C: community relations is a key component of the ability to operate.

rationales.A: Broader than regulatory.
rationales.B: Central to social license.
rationales.C:
Full statement: community relations is a key component of the ability to operate — community programs, workforce transition support, transparent handling of environmental impacts (like waste dumping), and lobbying for constructive solutions all support the fund's ongoing acceptance in the community.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of PE: We're going to design new community programs around that. We'll try to make a positive impact first. We know that cutting employees is sensitive. But by helping many more people than we let go and by giving employees proper training so they have the skills for whatever they're doing next, we're going to be part of a sensible transition. Head of Equities: That's going to be critical. Community relations is a key component of our social license to operate.' The framing bakes ESG into ongoing operational strategy.

drills.los_text: analyze and evaluate the financial and non-financial risk exposures in the portfolio strategy of an institutional investor
drills.reading_name: Case Study in Portfolio Management: Institutional (SWF)

questions.stem: The reading identifies R-SWF risk exposures categories most relevant to its portfolio. Which is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: commodity trading day-trading risk.
options.B: market/currency risk in frontier markets.
options.C: climate/environmental risk over 25-year horizons.

rationales.A: Correct. R-SWF is a long-term investor holding non-tradable direct investments, not a day-trading commodity operation. Relevant risk exposures include: (1) market/currency risk especially in frontier markets (both Sunnyland and Atsui); (2) climate/environmental risk on long-horizon infrastructure (sea-level rise, extreme heat); (3) political/reputational risks around labor practices and pollution (ABC); (4) macro/traffic risks (pandemic, recession); (5) interest rate risk on project debt.
rationales.B: This is relevant.
rationales.C: This is relevant.

questions.stem: The *3-year assessment* of ABC *most likely* highlights the reputational risk as:

options.A: The three-year ABC assessment treats reputational risk as unimportant relative to financial metrics.
options.B: the biggest concern — 'we don't want to end up in the newspaper firing people during a recession, polluting the river, threatening endangered species, and being rather petty about soap'.
options.C: The assessment treats reputational risk as fully measurable in dollar terms alone.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reputation is elevated to the CENTRAL concern.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the biggest concern — 'we don't want to end up in the newspaper firing people during a recession, polluting the river, threatening endangered species, and being rather petty about soap'; the investment could have outsized negative impact despite small size.

Correct. Per the 3-year discussion: 'Head of Risk: The reputational risk is a big concern. We don't want to end up in the newspaper firing people during a recession, polluting the river, threatening endangered species, and being rather petty about soap. Head of Infrastructure: True on all counts. Head of Risk: Ladies and gentlemen, the writing is on the wall. I propose we exit this investment as soon as possible, if we can.' The CIO adds: 'a small investment can have an outsized negative impact on us if we don't manage the risk properly.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concern is qualitative and broad — headlines about firing workers in a recession, polluting the river, and pressuring the community.

questions.stem: The reading emphasizes that a *small direct PE investment* can *most likely*:

options.A: always be ignored.
options.B: have no reputational risk.
options.C: have an outsized negative impact on the SWF's reputation if not properly managed.

rationales.A: Materiality goes beyond dollars.
rationales.B: Real reputational risk.
rationales.C:
Full statement: have an outsized negative impact on the SWF's reputation if not properly managed — despite representing only 0.05% of assets, the ABC investment attracts scrutiny due to R-SWF's sovereign status and headline risk of 'Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs in XYZ during a Recession.'

Correct. Per the reading, the CIO emphasizes: 'This is a rather small investment, of $25 million, but even a small investment can have an outsized negative impact on us if we don't manage the risk properly.' And later: 'Imagine the headline: "Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs in XYZ during a Recession."' The SWF's sovereign status raises the reputational stakes vs a conventional PE house.

questions.stem: The reading contrasts conventional PE houses vs SWFs on *headline risk* by *most likely* noting:

options.A: conventional PE houses can more easily cut jobs for purely financial reasons.
options.B: identical constraints.
options.C: SWFs have no reputational constraints.

rationales.A:
Full statement: conventional PE houses can more easily cut jobs for purely financial reasons; as a sovereign wealth fund, R-SWF has more complicated reputational considerations — hence 'Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs' headline risk.

Correct. Per the CIO: 'The conventional private equity houses can more easily cut jobs for purely financial reasons. However, as a sovereign wealth fund, it is more complicated for us. Imagine the headline: "Government of Ruritania Cuts Jobs in XYZ during a Recession." Head of Infrastructure: It's not a good look. Chief Investment Officer: It's not a good look. Right.' Sovereign status amplifies headline risk.
rationales.B: Substantial differences.
rationales.C: Sovereign status elevates constraints.

questions.stem: A *risk that was NOT well highlighted initially* but became critical later is *most likely*:

options.A: commodity price risk.
options.B: climate/environmental risk — dismissed at inception based on engineering assurances and 50/100-year flood scenarios.
options.C: operational risk only.

rationales.A: Not the primary emergent risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: climate/environmental risk — dismissed at inception based on engineering assurances and 50/100-year flood scenarios; three years later, sea-level rise and more frequent storms already impact operations; at 5 years, mitigating flood damage costs 50% higher than base case.

Correct. Per the case progression: Initial dismissal by Head of Infrastructure ('experts tell us... environmental risks are unlikely to materialize'). At 3 years: 'climate change has led to rising seas and more frequent storms.' At 5 years: 'higher costs (mitigating flood damage) vs forecast (50% higher than base case).' The Head of Equities' initial concern was validated over time.
rationales.C: Climate risk specifically.

questions.stem: R-SWF's *equities* team can sell out of a polluting company 'within the hour' vs long-term direct investments *most likely* highlights:

options.A: The equities-versus-direct comparison shows identical exit costs across the two investment types.
options.B: Long-term direct investments carry no exit friction beyond that of listed equities.
options.C: the difficulty of exiting long-term direct/private investments — 'our rather long-term horizon doesn't make it any easier for us to step away from an investment when the time comes'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The difference is enormous — public positions exit within the hour.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The comparison exists to highlight direct investments' exit DIFFICULTY — the long horizon does not make exiting any easier.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the difficulty of exiting long-term direct/private investments — 'our rather long-term horizon doesn't make it any easier for us to step away from an investment when the time comes'; contrasts with liquid listed equity exits.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Whether we've decided to make an exit on our own or because of outside pressure, our rather long-term horizon doesn't make it any easier for us to step away from an investment when the time comes. As a contrast, our Head of Equities was telling me before the meeting started that he wasn't too happy about how much one of his portfolio companies was polluting, and so he just went ahead and sold the position. It was a liquid investment in a public market, and he was done within the hour. That's a contrast we have to keep in mind.'

questions.stem: The Head of PE argues that ABC's problems *most likely* could be addressed by:

options.A: passing ABC to the distressed-asset team, engaging with management to change mindset (less profit-driven focus, sensible workforce transition e.g., half-salary training for 8 months to find another job), lobbying government on labor law flexibility, and stopping waste dumping.
options.B: The Head of PE argues that immediate exit is the only available response to ABC's problems, since minority investors have no channels for influencing portfolio company behavior.
options.C: The Head of PE concludes that nothing can be done about ABC's situation, recommending the fund write the position down and await maturity of the holding period.

rationales.A:
Full statement: passing ABC to the distressed-asset team, engaging with management to change mindset (less profit-driven focus, sensible workforce transition e.g., half-salary training for 8 months to find another job), lobbying government on labor law flexibility, and stopping waste dumping — trying to salvage before exiting.

Correct. Per the Head of PE's response: 'let's think about passing ABC over to the distressed-asset team before it becomes properly distressed... So we really need to figure out: Can we change how this business functions to manage that risk?... How do we motivate management to keep people inside and outside the plant happy? We have two board seats, and investing more money in modernization seems to make less sense now. Maybe we pay them a percentage — say, half their regular salary — while offering them good training and assistance for eight months to find another job. At the same time, we'd convince management to shift to a less profit-driven focus.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exit is one option among several — remediation comes first.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The proposed program is active: distressed-asset team involvement, management engagement on mindset, and workforce transition planning.

questions.stem: The reading emphasizes that a lobbying strategy in Atsui *most likely* leverages:

options.A: The Atsui lobbying strategy leverages financial threats against the government.
options.B: the framing that if the government's rigid labor laws prevent cutting 50% of the workforce, all 100% of the jobs may be at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20%.
options.C: No lobbying is possible in Atsui given the rigid labor law framework.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The approach is reasoned framing, not threats.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the framing that if the government's rigid labor laws prevent cutting 50% of the workforce, all 100% of the jobs may be at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20% — government doesn't want factory shutdown, so there may be room for negotiated conclusion.

Correct. Per Head of Equities: 'If we do try to salvage the situation and continue with our investment, there's a path that involves the government. Our pitch should be that if there are legal roadblocks for cutting 50% of the jobs, you might be putting 100% of the jobs at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20%. The government doesn't want the factory to shut down because of its rigid labor laws, so there may well be room for a more, let's say, negotiated conclusion.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Lobbying is an option — framed as: rigid rules blocking a 50% workforce cut may put 100% of the jobs at risk if the company fails.

questions.stem: *AOG's rumored contract renegotiation* is *most likely* a risk R-SWF should:

options.A: R-SWF should ignore AOG's rumored contract renegotiation as immaterial.
options.B: R-SWF should escalate the AOG renegotiation rumor directly to arbitration.
options.C: handle with a positive collaborative approach — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. An operator's dissatisfaction is a genuine operational risk.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The preferred posture is collaborative — a disgruntled operator harms the asset; happy employees and travelers make a better airport.
rationales.C:
Full statement: handle with a positive collaborative approach — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic'; if AOG activates break clause, R-SWF is responsible for finding new operator; wait for delay talks to resolve then collaborate.

Correct. Per the Head of Infrastructure: 'We all signed a well-structured agreement, and that affords us some decent protection against any meddling in the fee structure, though there are break clauses if anything gets too out of line. Still, there are incentives built into the concession agreement to make sure everyone wins to a greater or lesser extent when traffic goes up. Equally, we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic... AOG is a strong global operator. If they did activate a break clause in two or three years' time, that lands us with a responsibility we really don't want, which is finding a new operator. We're still satisfied with their cooperation. I recommend seeing how talks over the delays play out.'

questions.stem: The 5-year outcome for the ABC investment *most likely*:

options.A: was sold to an international beverage company for $27M (avoiding diplomatic and reputational risk).
options.B: was a total loss.
options.C: underwent write-off.

rationales.A:
Full statement: was sold to an international beverage company for $27M (avoiding diplomatic and reputational risk) — the buyer had been exporting to Atsui and wanted a local production hedge against currency weakness (natural FX hedge).

Correct. Per the Year 5 update: 'The PE team was able to avoid a diplomatic crisis and reputational risk damage by finding a buyer for the 35% stake. They sold the full position at $27 million. The stake was sold to an international beverage company that had been exporting to Atsui. The company's sales had been adversely affected by a weaker Atsui currency. Thus, producing locally is advantageous because it provides a natural foreign exchange hedge.'
rationales.B: Not lost.
rationales.C: Sold, not written off.

questions.stem: R-SWF's *asset allocation over 3 years* *most likely* shows:

options.A: flat AUM.
options.B: AUM growth from $50B to $56B.
options.C: AUM decline.

rationales.A: Growth occurred.
rationales.B:
Full statement: AUM growth from $50B to $56B — still outperforming benchmark overall but outperformance dulled by difficulties with some assets, primarily real estate (commercial real estate underperformed).

Correct. Per the 3-year meeting: 'In those three years, AUM have grown by $6 billion. We're still outperforming our overall benchmark, but our outperformance has been dulled by difficulties with some assets, primarily real estate, because commercial real estate has underperformed. So that's hurt us a little bit, but as ever, we are long-term investors.'
rationales.C: AUM grew.

questions.stem: *Political risk* in Sunnyland was assessed as *most likely* low because:

options.A: Political risk in Sunnyland was never assessed because no political risk exists in island nations.
options.B: Political risk was assessed as low because Sunnyland's authoritarian government guarantees stability.
options.C: past foreign investment (mostly tourism-side hotels) has been reasonable, government-to-government relations are good, an island nation faces less political interference from neighbors, and R-SWF was approached as a 'fellow government institution'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The assessment was made — and reasoned.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The low rating rested on a track record of reasonable treatment of foreign investment, good government-to-government relations, and the island's context — not authoritarian stability.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of PE: Investment in Sunnyland has mostly been on the tourism side. There's a mixture of three- and five-star hotels, so major international hotel operators are around. And they're still arriving, but they feel the transportation bottleneck. Those who are there and the ones who are thinking about coming in are happy about the airport project. Head of Infrastructure: I haven't heard any horror stories about investors in Sunnyland getting burned because of unfair rule changes. Plus, relations are good. The Sunnyland authorities approached us as a fellow government institution. CIO: One nice thing about an island nation is that it is an island. There's less political interference from the neighbours.'

questions.stem: R-SWF's *initial dismissal of currency risk* for ABC (Atsui) *most likely* proved to be:

options.A: wrong — a currency devaluation was anticipated at 3-year update.
options.B: correct.
options.C: irrelevant.

rationales.A:
Full statement: wrong — a currency devaluation was anticipated at 3-year update; Head of Risk's initial concern about frontier market currencies moving 'wildly against the dollar' was validated.

Correct. Per the reading, Head of PE initially said 'I'm not stressed about it. When it comes to me and most other visitors to Atsui, we're using US dollars.' Head of Risk warned currencies 'can move wildly against the dollar, turning a really good investment into a really bad investment.' At 3 years: 'a currency devaluation is anticipated.' At 5 years: the buyer wanted local production as natural FX hedge against weaker currency, confirming devaluation occurred.
rationales.B: Not correct.
rationales.C: Very relevant.

questions.stem: The R-SWF *risk factor analysis* framework *most likely* covers:

options.A: only equity risk.
options.B: exposure to equity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, inflation risk, currency risk, and liquidity risk.
options.C: only quantitative analysis.

rationales.A: Broader coverage.
rationales.B:
Full statement: exposure to equity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, inflation risk, currency risk, and liquidity risk — quantitative modeling and qualitative risk assessments (including potential reputational risk from certain types of investments).

Correct. Per the reading's ERM framework: 'Institutional investors typically perform risk factor analysis to better understand the fund's risk exposures, such as exposure to equity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, inflation risk, currency risk, and liquidity risk. This analysis includes both quantitative modeling and qualitative risk assessments. Quantitative tools may involve sophisticated risk management systems based on returns or holdings, scenario analysis, and stress testing. Other risks are more qualitative in nature, such as potential reputational risk from certain types of investments.'
rationales.C: Both quant and qual.

questions.stem: R-SWF's *risk mitigation actions* over the course of the case study *most likely* include:

options.A: R-SWF took no risk mitigation actions over the course of the case study, allowing both direct investments to run unmanaged from acquisition through exit.
options.B: R-SWF's only mitigation tool across the case study was litigation against counterparties, pursuing contract enforcement through the courts at each sign of trouble.
options.C: hedging (interest rate risk for airport), renegotiation and collaboration (AOG contract), ESG engagement (community programs, workforce transition), impact investing framing, secondary sale (ABC exit at $27M to strategic buyer), reputational risk management.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Actions were numerous and varied.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Litigation barely features; hedging, contract renegotiation and collaboration, and ESG engagement carried the mitigation program.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading, R-SWF's risk management actions across the case include: (1) interest rate hedging for Sunnyland airport project; (2) constructive negotiation with AOG (positive collaboration on delays and concession extension); (3) ESG engagement with ABC (workforce transition, environmental cleanup); (4) reframing ABC as impact investment; (5) sale of ABC stake at $27M to international beverage buyer; (6) proactive climate risk mitigation (drainage design). Risk management is an ongoing process, not a single action.

drills.los_text: discuss various methods to manage the risks that arise on long-term direct investments of an institutional investor
drills.reading_name: Case Study in Portfolio Management: Institutional (SWF)

questions.stem: For the Sunnyland airport, R-SWF's *currency risk management* is *most likely* based on:

options.A: structural mitigation: 70% revenue as USD airport charges (via IATA).
options.B: no management.
options.C: extensive derivatives hedging.

rationales.A:
Full statement: structural mitigation: 70% revenue as USD airport charges (via IATA); 30% commercial revenue priced in USD (targeting international tourists); $300M debt USD-denominated; overall alignment between revenue and debt currency.

Correct. Per the reading's dialogue, R-SWF manages Sunnyland FX risk via structural currency alignment: revenue side (70% airport charges paid in USD via IATA + 30% commercial priced in USD for international tourists) matches debt-side (USD-denominated). No derivative hedging is described.
rationales.B: Structural mitigation exists.
rationales.C: Structural, not derivative.

questions.stem: For managing Sunnyland *interest rate risk* on borrowing, R-SWF *most likely* can:

options.A: do nothing.
options.B: hedge its interest rate exposure.
options.C: ignore the exposure.

rationales.A: Interest rate is hedgeable.
rationales.B:
Full statement: hedge its interest rate exposure — since interest rate exposure is easily hedged via swaps and other derivatives, unlike currency risk in frontier markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'R-SWF can manage the risk that actual future (borrowing) interest rates will be higher than forecast by hedging its interest rate exposure for the Sunnyland airport project.' Interest rate risk on developed-market borrowings is straightforward to hedge via IR swaps and similar instruments.
rationales.C: Hedgeable.

questions.stem: For managing *ABC's carbonated beverage trend risk* (health foods trend), the recommended approach is *most likely*:

options.A: The recommended response to the health-foods trend is immediate exit from the ABC position.
options.B: The recommended response is doubling down exclusively on carbonated beverages.
options.C: develop new healthy alternatives (leverage experience producing natural mango drink, expand to sports drinks and juices).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Exit is premature; product adaptation comes first.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Concentration in the declining category is the risk itself — the path is healthy alternatives, leveraging the natural mango drink experience into sports drinks and juices.
rationales.C:
Full statement: develop new healthy alternatives (leverage experience producing natural mango drink, expand to sports drinks and juices) — differentiate to increase market share as consumer preferences shift.

Correct. Per the guideline answer: 'R-SWF can manage the risk of carbonated beverages falling out of favor due to an increasing preference for health foods by working to develop new healthy alternatives to carbonated, presumably sugar-filled drinks. As the production facility expands its ability to produce product, ABC could focus its new product development on healthy alternatives. The company can leverage its experience producing such beverages given the success of its natural mango drink in order to differentiate itself and increase market share.'

questions.stem: For managing *ABC's environmental damage* (river dumping), the reading's recommended actions are *most likely*:

options.A: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts.
options.B: The recommended action on ABC's river dumping is to ignore the issue as immaterial to returns.
options.C: The recommended action is to increase production volume, accepting greater pollution as a cost of growth.

rationales.A:
Full statement: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts — 'in the long run, regulations catch up to you and the cost of pre-treatment or appropriate handling of waste is much lower than the penalties.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of PE: Ending that practice is an important piece of our talks with management, and so is removing incentives to cut corners... Head of Equities: Our experience in other developing nations as well as developed nations tells us that you'll save some costs in the short term with actions like dumping waste directly into water bodies, but in the long run, regulations catch up to you and the cost of pre-treatment or appropriate handling of waste is much lower than the penalties you get for taking such shortcuts.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The practice must end — reputational and community stakes make it material.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The direction is remediation: end the dumping, remove corner-cutting incentives, and reframe management's long-term thinking.

questions.stem: The *impact investing* framing for ABC is *most likely*:

options.A: The impact-investing framing for ABC is a device for reducing the fund's reporting obligations.
options.B: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river.
options.C: The impact framing means abandoning financial goals in favor of purely social objectives.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framing broadens the investment thesis rather than shrinking disclosure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river; can we inform their thinking?' The CIO explores whether investing has broader impact utility.

Correct. Per CIO: 'We talked about this being a learning experience. We also talked about it displaying aspects of impact investment. Maybe part of the value is in education. In some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river. Can we inform their thinking with the idea that wanting a beautiful river for fishing and enjoyment is a virtue and that it's not really that hard to dispose of waste properly? What can we intelligently say about impact?'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Impact COMPLEMENTS financial goals — education value around waste disposal and river stewardship in a developing market alongside returns.

questions.stem: For *workforce transition risk*, the Head of PE proposes *most likely*:

options.A: The Head of PE proposes immediate mass firing of ABC's factory workforce with no transition support.
options.B: The Head of PE proposes no support of any kind for transitioning workers.
options.C: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Abrupt mass termination is the reputational scenario the fund is trying to avoid.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The proposal is substantial: half salary plus eight months of training and job-search assistance, shifting management from a purely profit-driven posture.
rationales.C:
Full statement: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach; 'we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Maybe we pay them a percentage — say, half their regular salary — while offering them good training and assistance for eight months to find another job. At the same time, we'd convince management to shift to a less profit-driven focus.' Also: 'We know that cutting employees is sensitive. But by helping many more people than we let go and by giving employees proper training so they have the skills for whatever they're doing next, we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'

questions.stem: Institutional investors' *emergency action plan* for illiquid portfolios *most likely* includes:

options.A: what to liquidate — and in what order — in a crisis to meet cash flows and how to rebalance the portfolio in a crisis.
options.B: no plan needed.
options.C: only market predictions.

rationales.A:
Full statement: what to liquidate — and in what order — in a crisis to meet cash flows and how to rebalance the portfolio in a crisis; sharing it with the board to get buy-in and mitigate risk of board pressure for suboptimal short-term decisions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Institutional investors should put in place an emergency action plan. Such an action plan should include what to liquidate — and in what order — in a crisis to meet cash flows and how to rebalance the portfolio in a crisis. Having such a plan in place can help avoid the risk of panicking in a crisis. Sharing the emergency action plan with the board to get buy-in can also help when a crisis occurs and mitigate the risk of board members pressuring the investment team to make sub-optimal short-term decisions.'
rationales.B: Plan is critical.
rationales.C: Action-oriented plan.

questions.stem: The *five steps in developing a liquidity management plan* (Exhibit 5) are *most likely*:

options.A: same as market forecasting.
options.B: (1) Establish liquidity risk parameters.
options.C: unspecified.

rationales.A: Different framework.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) Establish liquidity risk parameters; (2) Assess the liquidity of current portfolio and monitor evolution over time; (3) Develop a cash flow model and project future cash flows; (4) Stress test liquidity needs and cash flow projections; (5) Develop an emergency action plan.

Correct. Per Exhibit 5, the five steps are: '1. Establish liquidity risk parameters. 2. Assess the liquidity of current portfolio, and monitor the evolution over time. 3. Develop a cash flow model and project future cash flows. 4. Stress test liquidity needs and cash flow projections. 5. Develop an emergency action plan.' This provides a structured approach to liquidity management for long-term institutional investors.
rationales.C: Well defined.

questions.stem: For managing *illiquid-portfolio exit challenges*, R-SWF's tools *most likely* include:

options.A: no exit tools available.
options.B: only public market exits.
options.C: secondary sales (as done with ABC.

rationales.A: Multiple tools.
rationales.B: Not public exits.
rationales.C:
Full statement: secondary sales (as done with ABC — sold to strategic buyer for $27M), passing to distressed-asset team (proposed for ABC), reducing commitments to further vintage-year investments, and being prepared for longer exit horizons than public equity.

Correct. Per the reading, R-SWF's tools for managing illiquid exit include: (1) secondary sales (the ABC exit at $27M to international beverage buyer); (2) reallocation to distressed-asset teams within the SWF; (3) reducing commitments to new vintage year investments; (4) commitment pacing to control future allocations. The reading also notes: 'These actions may involve hedging, rebalancing, and secondary sales or in the case of illiquid investments, reducing commitments.'

questions.stem: For *contract renegotiation risk with AOG* (airport operator), R-SWF's approach *most likely* is:

options.A: positive collaboration — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator'.
options.B: immediate arbitration.
options.C: immediate contract termination.

rationales.A:
Full statement: positive collaboration — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator'; if delays are found to be government's liability, request concession extension and 'sit down with AOG to positively collaborate on retooling the whole picture.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of Infrastructure: We all signed a well-structured agreement, and that affords us some decent protection... Equally, we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic. We haven't been formally approached about this, but let's not dismiss it out of hand just because we have a contract we can hide behind. AOG is a strong global operator. If they did activate a break clause in two or three years' time, that lands us with a responsibility we really don't want, which is finding a new operator. We're still satisfied with their cooperation. I recommend seeing how talks over the delays play out, and if we find that the government is liable for the delay, we'll request an extension to the concession and then sit down with AOG to positively collaborate on retooling the whole picture.'
rationales.B: Collaborative approach.
rationales.C: Not termination.

questions.stem: *Construction cost overrun risk* for Sunnyland airport is *most likely* mitigated via:

options.A: Construction cost overrun risk for the airport carries no mitigation in the deal structure.
options.B: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor.
options.C: Overrun risk is managed through cost-plus contracting that reimburses the builder's actual expenses.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The structure addresses it contractually.
rationales.B:
Full statement: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor; ±10% CapEx sensitivity applied in modeling.

Correct. Per the reading: 'There are some sensitivities around CapEx, and we're looking to manage this risk through a fixed-price contract, the language of which says that whatever penalties we'd face for delayed or subpar construction will be passed down to the contractor. We've applied a ±10% sensitivity around that.' Fixed-price contract structure passes cost overrun risk to contractor, protecting R-SWF's IRR from CapEx uncertainty.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cost-plus would EXPOSE the project to overruns; the tendered FIXED-PRICE contract passes delay and quality penalties to the contractor.

questions.stem: For *concession fee/quality standards risk*, R-SWF's protection is *most likely*:

options.A: R-SWF has no protection regarding concession fees or quality standards at the airport.
options.B: The concession agreement contains no contractual protections of any kind.
options.C: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Protections exist and are contractual.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The concession agreement embeds quality and performance standards across design, construction, and development — including timely delivery.
rationales.C:
Full statement: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations; break clauses for out-of-line performance.

Correct. Per Memo 1B and Head of Infrastructure: 'Concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations, respectively... We all signed a well-structured agreement, and that affords us some decent protection against any meddling in the fee structure, though there are break clauses if anything gets too out of line.' Contractual quality/performance standards drive risk sharing.

questions.stem: For managing *reputational risk* proactively, R-SWF's ABC investment approach *most likely* includes:

options.A: 'we're not just investing and then forgetting things'.
options.B: no proactive steps.
options.C: public denial only.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 'we're not just investing and then forgetting things' — before modernization starts, research what issues are in the minds of the people of Atsui, design new community programs, make a positive impact first, and support employees through training.

Correct. Per Head of PE: 'We're not just investing and then forgetting things, folks. We're going to be proactive. Before modernization starts, we're going to do some research that shows us what issues are in the minds of all the people of Atsui, not just our workers, and we're going to design new community programs around that. We'll try to make a positive impact first. We know that cutting employees is sensitive. But by helping many more people than we let go and by giving employees proper training so they have the skills for whatever they're doing next, we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'
rationales.B: Proactive approach envisioned.
rationales.C: Not denial.

questions.stem: For *management/governance risk* in ABC (only 35% minority stake), R-SWF's controls *most likely* include:

options.A: With only a 35% minority stake in ABC, R-SWF holds no governance controls and must accept whatever decisions the founder makes without board representation.
options.B: two board seats (from 5 to 7 board members), structured incentives so founder aligns with growth and good oversight (founder retains 65% skin in the game), ongoing due diligence, and being the ones implementing modernization for enhanced monitoring.
options.C: R-SWF exercises no oversight of ABC's management whatsoever, having negotiated purely economic rights with no board participation or incentive alignment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Minority position notwithstanding, controls were negotiated.
